symbols: throne — Mary as Sedes Sapientiae, the Seat of Wisdom bearing the incarnate Word
orb — sovereignty and dominion over the cosmos
architectural throne with columns — the Church as the house of God
medallions in upper corners — saints or angels witnessing the theophany
Christ Child on Mary's lap — the Incarnation, God accessible through human motherhood
description: A rectangular bronze or metal relief plaque showing the Virgin Mary seated on an ornate architectural throne with arched back and flanking columns topped with spherical finials. She holds the Christ Child on her left knee. The Child appears to hold a book or scroll and reaches toward His mother. Two circular medallions with figures are set in the upper corners. The throne has elaborate architectural details with small arches and decorative elements at the base. The relief is rendered in dark patinated metal with a beaded border.
theological_description: This metal relief of the enthroned Virgin and Child (Sedes Sapientiae) represents one of the most ancient and theologically significant icon types. Mary is depicted not merely as a mother but as the living Throne of God — the one in whose womb the Word took flesh and through whom God became accessible to humanity. The elaborate architectural throne symbolizes the Church itself, built upon the foundation of the Incarnation. The Christ Child holding a book identifies Him as the Logos, the divine Word and Wisdom. This image type emerged in early Christian art from the convergence of two theological affirmations: Mary as Theotokos (God-bearer, affirmed at the Council of Ephesus, 431) and Christ as Pantocrator. The frontal, hieratic presentation removes the scene from historical narrative into eternal theological truth.

scripture_refs.reason: The Holy Spirit will come upon you — Mary as the dwelling place of God, the living throne
Heaven is My throne — yet God chose to sit enthroned on Mary's lap in the Incarnation
Wisdom present at creation — Christ as divine Wisdom held by the Seat of Wisdom
Elizabeth's exclamation: the mother of my Lord — the theological basis for Mary as Theotokos
